food_subgroup: Noodle soups with fish and vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tomato fish noodle soup; closest parent 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_00002572) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Noodle soup) is the closest parent category; no specific tomato fish noodle soup code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for tomato fish noodle soup as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of tomato fish noodle soup weighing approximately 500 g, comprising noodles, fish slices, tomato broth, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Fish noodle soup (estimated from component analysis: wheat noodles, white fish, tomato broth) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 168936), cooked white fish (FDC ID 175168), and tomato-based broth; supplemented by HPB Singapore Foodo data for comparable fish noodle soup dishes.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Sunlit Tomato Fish Noodle Soup' exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model: approximately 35% cooked wheat noodles, 20% white fish fillet (steamed/poached), 40% tomato-based fish broth, and 5% garnishes (spring onion, ginger). Values cross-referenced against HPB Singapore Foodo fish noodle soup entries and USDA SR Legacy data for individual components. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and mixed noodle soup dishes — analogous estimate based on 'Wheat noodles, boiled' (GI ~52) from the University of Sydney GI Database; adjusted for dilution effect of broth and fish protein content.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tomato fish noodle soup. GI of 52 is estimated using boiled wheat noodles (GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database) as the primary glycemic driver. The presence of fish protein and tomato-based broth is expected to moderately attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 46.0) / 100 = 23.9. Serving carbohydrate: 9.2 g per 100g × 500g serving = 46.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as a 500 g serving provides approximately 1900 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard 500 g bowl delivers a glycemic load of approximately 24, which is moderate; reducing noodle portion and increasing fish and vegetable content can lower the glycemic impact of this meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from broth and fish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content (~1900 mg per serving) is the primary concern; the lean white fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, but sodium reduction through low-sodium broth preparation is strongly recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato Fish Noodle Soup (番茄鱼汤面) is a Chinese-style noodle soup dish consisting of tender wheat noodles served in a tangy, umami-rich tomato-based fish broth, topped with slices of lightly poached or steamed white fish fillet, spring onions, and ginger. The dish is popular in Singapore's Chinese food scene and is commonly found at noodle stalls and casual restaurants. The broth is typically prepared by simmering fish bones and tomatoes together, yielding a naturally sweet and slightly acidic soup base that is both light and flavourful. The texture contrasts soft noodles with delicate, flaky fish, while the tomato broth provides a refreshing acidity. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-calorie meal (~360 kcal per 500 g bowl) with a good protein contribution from the fish (~29 g per serving), low saturated fat, and a meaningful carbohydrate load from the noodles. Sodium content is elevated due to the broth, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic load is moderate (~24 per serving), driven primarily by the wheat noodles. The dish is halal-friendly and dairy-free, making it accessible to a wide range of dietary preferences in Singapore's multicultural food environment.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model using USDA FoodData Central data for individual ingredients (cooked wheat noodles, white fish, tomato broth) and cross-referenced against HPB Singapore Foodo comparable fish noodle soup entries. GI is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the estimation approach rather than a direct source match.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this estimated dish record. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in lean white fish and wheat noodles per USDA FoodData Central — confirmed zero by component source data. 5. The food_name has been normalised from the field survey menu name 'Sunlit Tomato Fish Noodle Soup' to the canonical English form 'Tomato Fish Noodle Soup'; the original menu name is preserved as an alias. 6. Serving size of 500 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant bowl of noodle soup in Singapore; actual portion may vary by restaurant. 7. Sodium content is high (~380 mg/100g; ~1900 mg per 500 g serving) due to fish broth seasoning; individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should exercise caution. 8. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in approved sources. 9. Glycemic load per serving = (52 × 46.0) / 100 = 23.9, where 46.0 g is the estimated carbohydrate content of a 500 g serving (9.2 g/100g × 500 g). 10. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are estimated from USDA component data and carry medium confidence.
